rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute diffuse otitis externa is a common, localised external-canal infection that is telehealth/GP-manageable. Although the pain is severe and distressing, there is no systemic toxicity, no diabetes/immunosuppression and no feature of invasive complication, so this is not an emergency. The correct tier is T2 (prompt GP or telehealth prescriber within 24-48 hours) to obtain prescription topical antibiotic/steroid drops and optimise analgesia, plus clear safety-netting. The source note's 'urgent after-hours' framing reflects an in-person clinician's MBS billing rationale for a home visit, not true medical acuity; escalating this to same-day ED/urgent care would be over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading redness or swelling behind the ear over the mastoid, or the ear being pushed forward/outward
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests mastoiditis or peri-auricular cellulitis — needs urgent in-person assessment and likely IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Go to an emergency department the same day if you notice redness or swelling spreading to the bone behind your ear, or your ear starts being pushed forward.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Facial weakness/droop, or severe deep boring pain out of proportion, especially with cranial-nerve symptoms
escalation_edges.rationale: Points to necrotising (malignant) otitis externa or nerve involvement — urgent ENT assessment and imaging required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Seek urgent same-day care (emergency department) if you develop facial weakness, or deep severe pain that is out of proportion — particularly important if you have diabetes or a weakened immune system.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever, rigors, feeling systemically very unwell, neck stiffness, confusion or focal neurological deficit
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates possible systemic or intracranial spread of infection.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Go to an emergency department urgently if you develop a high fever with shakes, feel very unwell in yourself, or get a stiff neck, confusion or any weakness.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after 48-72 hours of topical therapy, or symptoms worsening
escalation_edges.rationale: Non-response may indicate a wick is needed, a resistant or fungal organism, or the diagnosis needs review — GP reassessment and possible ear swab.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your ear is no better after 2-3 days of the drops, or it is getting worse, see your GP for review and a possible ear swab.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe headache with vomiting, new neurological deficit or collapse
escalation_edges.rationale: Rare intracranial complication of ear infection — a medical emergency needing an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 immediately if you get a sudden severe headache with vomiting, weakness, or you collapse.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: If the patient were diabetic or immunocompromised (not the case here)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The threshold for necrotising otitis externa falls sharply — such a patient with severe otitis externa should have same-day in-person ENT/GP assessment rather than telehealth-only management, and a lower threshold for imaging.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is sleep-deprived and distressed
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can obtain and correctly self-instil the ear drops, and has someone to check on them or a clear plan to seek help if they deteriorate overnight.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like an infection of the outer ear canal. Arrange a prompt appointment with your GP or a telehealth prescriber within the next day or two to get prescription antibiotic/steroid ear drops, and in the meantime add regular ibuprofen to your paracetamol (kept within safe limits) for the pain. Keep the ear dry and do not use cotton buds.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a prompt GP or telehealth prescriber appointment within 24-48 hours
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the pain is intolerable overnight, an after-hours GP or nurse-on-call line can advise on analgesia and arrange earlier review; go to an emergency department only if any of the red-flag warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive telehealth diagnosis of acute otitis externa; topical antibiotic/steroid drops recommended, analgesia optimised, red flags safety-netted. GP to confirm with otoscopy, consider aural toilet/ear wick and swab if refractory.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with no prescription therapy and no safety-netting for the necrotising/mastoiditis red flags.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A severe canal infection is left under-treated and a developing necrotising otitis externa or mastoiditis is missed because no warning signs were given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to routine GP review over 1-2 weeks despite severe analgesia-refractory pain needing prompt prescription therapy.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a localised, uncomplicated external ear infection to the emergency department or calling 000.
